citation_sentences: The TEM micrographs of the γ-austenite in the annealed steel strips are shown in Fig. 11. The superlattice reflection spots appear in the SADP along [110] zone axis taken from the γ-austenite. And the DF images using (001) indicate that a large number of nano-scale κ-carbides in the annealed 0Cr and 3Cr steel strips. The measured volume fraction and average size of the κ-carbides in the annealed 0Cr steel strip reach 7% and 8 nm, respectively. And, in the annealed 3Cr steel strip, they are 4% and 4 nm, respectively. It can be concluded that, annealing treatment at 600 °C promotes the precipitation and growth of κ-carbides, which leads to an increase in the yield strength, as shown in Fig. 3. According to Fig. 11, the Cr addition makes the size and number of κ- carbides decrease significantly in the annealed steel strips. The reason should be that addition of Cr results in expanding the solid solubility of Al and C in the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel [28,29], which reduces the driving force of the formation of κ-carbide for a given Al and C content. Therefore, the higher the Cr content, the less the κ-carbide precipitation. And the yield strength of the annealed Cr-containing steel strips also decreases.

It is should be noted that the annealing treatment after cold rolling also promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides, but the effect of the newly formed κ-carbides on the strain hardening rate (Fig. 3 (c) and (d)) is weaker than that of the as-cast steel strips (Fig. 9 (c) and (d)). After cold rolling, there are a large number of dislocations and slip bands in the steel strips, as shown in Fig. 10. The annealing treatment after cold rolling promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides, but does not eliminate these dislocations and slip bands, as shown in Fig. 12. At early strain stage, these deformation microstructures result in high yield stress of the steel strips, which makes dislocations easier to shear κ-carbides.

With the strain progressing, the dislocations are constantly generated. Due to the glide plane softening, the distorted κ-carbides are continuously sheared by a large number of dislocations, which further reduce the pinning effect of κ-carbides [3,10,14]. Therefore, the effect of κ-carbides as an energy barrier becomes increasingly weaker. And at final strain stage of the 0Cr and 6Cr steel strips, the difference in strain hardening rate caused by κ-carbides becomes small, as shown in Fig. 3.

With respect to the reason for that the strain hardening rate of annealed 0Cr steel strip shows a clear double hump like feature shown in Fig. 3 (d). According to the above analysis, the first hump of the strain hardening rate is related to the slip-band refinement, and the second is due to the formation of microbands. After the beginning of plastic deformation, the generation of slip bands leads to the first hump of the strain hardening rate. As the slip band spacing reaches the critical value, the strain hardening rate decreases. Under high strain condition, the generation of microbands results in the second hump of the strain hardening rate. As strain increases further, the necking occurs and then specimen fractures by plastic instability. It is emphasized that the number of nano-scale κ-carbides plays a crucial role in the strain hardening rate at the early strain stage as discussed above. When the number of κ-carbides is great, the early strain hardening rate caused by slip bands is low and similar to that caused by microband hardening level. This makes the strain hardening rate of the anneal 0Cr steel strip show a clear double hump like feature as shown in Fig. 3 (d). But when number of κ-carbides is small as the Cr-containing steel strips, the early strain hardening rate caused by slip bands is obviously higher than that caused by microband hardening, which presents a gradually decrease trend in whole tensile process and the hump like feature becomes weaker.

caption: Fig. 4. TEM micrographs of γ-austenite (a–c) and δ-ferrite (d–f) in the as-cast 0Cr steel strip: (a) BF image of γ-austenite; (b) DF from (001) ; (c) the SADP along [110] zone axis from the γ-austenite (hkl: austenite; hkl: κ-carbide); (d) BF image of δ-ferrite; (e) DF from (002) superlattice reflection of the ordered phase; (f) the SADP along [110] zone axis from the δ-ferrite (hkl: ferrite; hkl: DO3).

citation_sentences: TEM was performed to elucidate the effect of Cr addition on the nano-scale precipitated phase in the low-density steel strips. TEM micrographs in Fig. 4 show the precipitated phases in γ-austenite and δ-ferrite of the as-cast Cr-free steel strip. Fig. 4 (a) is a bright-field (BF) image taken from the γ-austenite, in which some scattered spots are present in γ-austenite. Correspondingly, the dark-field (DF) image using (001) indicates that the nanoparticles are distributed in γ-austenite, as shown in Fig. 4 (b). Fig. 4 (c) and (f) are two selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) taken from the γ-austenite and δ-ferrite along [110] zone axis, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 (c), the superlattice reflection spots indicate that the ordered κ-carbides are present in the γ-austenite with the orientation relationship of [110]γ//[110]κ [13,18]. The measured volume fraction and average size of κ-carbides in the γ-austenite are 3% and 6 nm, respectively. The measured lattice parameter of the κ-carbides and γ-austenite in the as-cast 0Cr steel strip is 0.387 nm and 0.375 nm, respectively. The ideal stoichiometry of the κ-carbide is (Fe, Mn)3AlC, which has a perovskite crystal [5]. In this crystal structure, Fe or Mn atoms occupy the cornered positions, Al atoms occupy the position of the face center, and C is in the center of the cube

crystal structure (½, ½, ½) [5]. As shown in Fig. 4 (d), the irregularly shaped δ-ferrite insets in γ-austenite, and the DF image using (002) shows clear ordering of the δ-ferrite, as shown in Fig. 4 (e). According to the diffuse superlattice reflection spots appeared in Fig. 4 (f), the ordered phase in the δ-ferrite is DO3 phase with size of 26 ± 3 nm [36,38,39]. For the SADP of δ-ferrite in the Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, when h, k and l (the Miller indices of the g! vector) are unmixed [30,38,39], the reflections belong to DO3 phase. It means that in SADP of ferrite, DO3 reflection occurs where h, k and l (the Miller indices of the g! vector) are all odd or even. Therefore, the δ-ferrite contains DO3 ordered phase. M.C. Ha et al. [39] and K.T. Park et al. [36] reported that some domains bounded by the swirled boundaries occurred in the DF image using (002) when B2 and DO3 coexisted in the δ-ferrite. However, the similar morphology does not occur in the DF images using (002), and it is confirmed that the precipitate in the ferrite is mainly DO3 phase.

citation_sentences: As shown in Fig. 9 (c), the strain hardening rates of the CR20% steel strips decrease gradually with increasing strain. The addition of Cr also increases the strain hardening rate of the CR20% steel strips. Comparing with the CR20% steel strips, annealing makes the strain hardening rates of the CR20% + annealed 0Cr and 3Cr steel strips clearly decrease at the early stage.

After cold rolling with 20% reduction, the strength of the Crcontaining steel strips is higher than that of Cr-free steel strips, as shown in Fig. 9. E. Welsch et al. [11] reported a clear correspondence between the slip band spacing and the stress required for deformation. The smaller the slip band spacing, the greater the stress required for deformation. In this work, there is a higher density of the slip bands in the Cr-containing cold-rolled steel strips than that of the Cr-free cold-rolled steel strips. The high-density slip bands significantly block the movement of dislocations and increase the deformation difficulty, which leads to an increase in strength of the Cr-containing steel strips. Thus, high density of the slip bands is responsible for the high strength of the Cr-containing cold-rolled steel strips.

The BF images of the γ-austenite in the CR20% + annealed 0Cr, 3Cr and 6Cr steel strips are shown in Fig. 12 (a), (c) and (e), respectively. The SADPs taken along [110] and [100] zone axis from the γ-austenite are shown in the inset of the BF images. It indicates that the annealing treatment at 600 °C also promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides in the CR20% steel strips. The DF images of κ-carbides using (001) shown in Fig. 12 (b) and (d) indicate that the κ-carbides are mainly precipitated at the dislocation entanglement in the CR20% + annealed 0Cr and 3Cr steel strips. But in the CR20% + annealed 6Cr steel strip, the size of κ-carbides is too small to observe in the DF image, as shown in Fig. 12 (f). Although annealing promotes the precipitation of κ- carbides, the yield strength of the CR20% + annealed steel strips does not significantly increase, as shown in Fig. 9. This can be attributed to the fact that the high density slip bands become the main strengthening factor for the cold-rolled steel strips.

It is should be noted that the annealing treatment after cold rolling also promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides, but the effect of the newly formed κ-carbides on the strain hardening rate (Fig. 3 (c) and (d)) is weaker than that of the as-cast steel strips (Fig. 9 (c) and (d)). After cold rolling, there are a large number of dislocations and slip bands in the steel strips, as shown in Fig. 10. The annealing treatment after cold rolling promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides, but does not eliminate these dislocations and slip bands, as shown in Fig. 12. At early strain stage, these deformation microstructures result in high yield stress of the steel strips, which makes dislocations easier to shear κ-carbides.

citation_sentences: To elucidate the reason for the difference in reinforcement effect of the cold-rolled steel strips, TEM was used to examine the microstructures of CR20% 0Cr, 3Cr and 6Cr steel strips. The BF images and the SADP taken from [110] zone axis of the γ-austenite in the three CR20% steel strips are shown in Fig. 10. According to these TEM micrographs, {111} planar slip bands are present in the austenite of the CR20% steel strips. Furthermore, there is a significant difference in the slip band spacing between the Cr-free and Crcontaining steel strips. The slip band spacing was measured at least three different regions in each specimen. The measured results show that the average spacing of the slip bands in 0Cr, 3Cr and 6Cr steel strips is 371 ± 47 nm, 100 ± 15 nm and 45 ± 13 nm, respectively. The difference in the slip band spacing should be responsible for the difference in reinforcement effect of the steel strips after cold rolling. The smaller slip band spacing, the stronger reinforcement effect after cold rolling.

It is should be noted that the annealing treatment after cold rolling also promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides, but the effect of the newly formed κ-carbides on the strain hardening rate (Fig. 3 (c) and (d)) is weaker than that of the as-cast steel strips (Fig. 9 (c) and (d)). After cold rolling, there are a large number of dislocations and slip bands in the steel strips, as shown in Fig. 10. The annealing treatment after cold rolling promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides, but does not eliminate these dislocations and slip bands, as shown in Fig. 12. At early strain stage, these deformation microstructures result in high yield stress of the steel strips, which makes dislocations easier to shear κ-carbides.

The SADPs taken along [110] and [100] zone axis from the γ-austenite are shown in the inset of the BF images. It indicates that the annealing treatment at 600 °C also promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides in the CR20% steel strips. The DF images of κ-carbides using (001) shown in Fig. 12 (b) and (d) indicate that the κ-carbides are mainly precipitated at the dislocation entanglement in the CR20% + annealed 0Cr and 3Cr steel strips. But in the CR20% + annealed 6Cr steel strip, the size of κ-carbides is too small to observe in the DF image, as shown in Fig. 12 (f). This can be attributed to the fact that the high density slip bands become the main strengthening factor for the cold-rolled steel strips.

It is should be noted that the annealing treatment after cold rolling also promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides, but the effect of the newly formed κ-carbides on the strain hardening rate (Fig. 3 (c) and (d)) is weaker than that of the as-cast steel strips (Fig. 9 (c) and (d)). caption: Fig. 13. TEM micrographs of the annealed 0Cr and 6Cr steel strips at a true strain of 0.075 during tensile deformation: (a) slip bands in 0Cr steel strips, (b) dislocation network structure in 0Cr steel strip, (c) slip bands in 6Cr steel strips, (d) scattered dislocations in 6Cr steel strips.

citation_sentences: The BF images of the annealed 0Cr and 6Cr steel strips at a true strain of 0.075 are shown in Fig. 13. At this strain stage, some slip bands are present in the austenite. As shown in Fig. 13 (a) and (c), the addition of 6Cr makes the spacing of the slip bands smaller than that of 0Cr steel strips at this strain stage. The higher density of slip bands caused by the same strain would result in higher strain hardening rate in the 6Cr steel strips, because it makes the dislocations glide more difficult [14,41,42]. A typical dislocation network structure is also present in the austenite of the 0Cr steel strip, as shown in Fig. 13 (b), which is similar to the microstructure in austenite-ferrite Fe-18.1Mn-9.6Al-0.65C steel at a true strain of 0.06 [9]. In addition, the dislocations between the slip bands become entangled and agglomerated in some regions of the 6Cr steel strip, as shown in Fig. 13 (d).
The slip bands and the dislocation arrays shown in Fig. 13 (a) and (c) indicate a typical planar slip characteristic [9], which is related to the

caption: Fig. 14. TEM micrographs of the annealed 0Cr and 6Cr steel strips after tensile fracture: (a) slip bands in 0Cr steel strips, (b) intersections between ferrite and microbands in 0Cr steel strips, (c) shearable grain boundary in 6Cr steel strips, (d) microbands in 6Cr steel strips.

citation_sentences: In addition, the dislocation entanglements occur at the phase boundary between ferrite and austenite, which can be attributed to the inconsistent deformation of ferrite and austenite during the deformation process. And the slip bands can extend through a grain boundary, and lead to many small steps at the grain boundary, as shown in Fig. 14 (c). The SADPs along [110] zone axis on both sides of the grain boundary indicate that this grain boundary is a low-angle grain boundary with a misorientation of 2.56°, as shown in Fig. 14 (c). The phenomenon is observed only at high strain and is rarely observed, which the reason is not clear.
